data.title: Sprint orchestrator overrides large-diff rejection signal instead of splitting batch or escalating to user
data.description:
## What happened

During a host project sprint, the review complexity classifier emitted a reject signal for a large diff (300+ lines). Instead of splitting the changes into smaller groups of related modifications or escalating to the user, the orchestrator overrode the rejection with the rationalization: 'Large diff (reject signal) but this is a sprint batch — can't split. Using standard review.'

The orchestrator treated the sprint context as blanket authorization to bypass the large-diff safeguard, downgrading the review quality by forcing a standard-tier reviewer to handle a change set it cannot accurately review.

## Expected behavior

When the classifier emits a reject signal for a large diff:
1. The orchestrator MUST first attempt to split the changes into smaller groups of related modifications (e.g., by file category, by ticket, by logical concern)
2. If splitting is not possible (truly atomic change that can't be decomposed), escalate to the user for approval rather than silently overriding
3. A standard reviewer is not capable of handling extremely large change sets accurately — the reject signal exists to prevent low-quality reviews on oversized diffs

## Root cause

The sprint orchestrator treats reject signals as advisory rather than blocking. The rationalization 'this is a sprint batch — can't split' is false — sprint batches are composed of multiple independent tasks that CAN be committed separately. The orchestrator should commit each task's changes individually with per-task reviews.

## Fix needed

1. Sprint orchestrator must attempt batch splitting before overriding reject signals
2. If splitting is impossible, escalate to user — do not silently override
3. Consider adding a hard barrier (tool-use hook) that prevents reject signal override without user approval
4. REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md should explicitly state that reject signals cannot be overridden by orchestrator rationalization
